title: Mild Cognitive Impairment Prediction
text:
Mild Cognitive Impairment Prediction


R Machine Learning Healthcare Classification




Author


James N. Hernandez




Published


2026









This project was completed as part of my graduate coursework in Data Science at the University of Minnesota.
The goal was to develop predictive models for identifying Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI) using demographic, clinical, and cognitive assessment variables. Multiple machine learning approaches were compared and evaluated using cross-validation and held-out testing data to assess predictive performance and interpretability.

title: Geographies of Deservingness
text:
Geographies of Deservingness


R Research Causal Inference Sociology




Author


James N. Hernandez




Published


May 2025






Website



Github








This project began as my undergraduate honors thesis in Sociology at Macalester College. I was interested in understanding how place influences asylum outcomes and whether broader political and socioeconomic conditions shape who is granted protection in the United States.
Using a cleaned dataset of approximately 36,000 asylum cases from 2020–2024, I evaluated multiple machine learning and statistical modeling approaches, including logistic regression, random forests, gradient boosting, support vector machines, and hierarchical logistic regression. The project examined how state-level characteristics—including political context, economic indicators, and nonprofit infrastructure—were associated with asylum approval decisions. Results suggested that wealthier states and states with stronger Democratic political representation were associated with higher approval probabilities, while nonprofit density emerged as an important predictor of favorable outcomes.

title: pokemon
text:
** UNDER CONSTRUCTION **
Pokemon Card Game is a multi-level memory card game developed in Java with kilt-graphics API to enhance concentration and critical thinking skills in 5-8 youth. The game has 3 levels of difficulty: easy (5 pairs, 10 attempts), medium (10 pairs, 20 attempts), and difficult (15 pairs, 30 attempts). The game begins with all cards flipped upside down. Upon clicking one of the cards, it flips face-side-up to reveal the pokemon it pertains to. The goal of the each round is to find the matching card, with 15 total pairs, for the card flipped. Upon the second card being clicked, if it is not a correct match, the cards will flip back over to hide the pokemon. However, if they match, the two cards will be removed from the screen. The goal of the game is to remove all pairs before the given time and under the limited number of attempts.

title: Projects
text:
Under Construction: I’m currently building out detailed pages for each project as a summer side project. Please check back soon!
A selection of research, data science, and statistical projects completed through coursework, research, and professional experiences.

 Default 




2026 James Hernandez



 Predicting COVID Status from Multi-Omics Data 

R Machine Learning Biostatistics

Evaluation and finetuning of various predictive models using, proteomic, metabolomic, and genomic clinical datasets to predict biomarkers of COVID-19.
Includes supervised, unsupervised, and neural network techniques.








2025 James Hernandez



 Geographies of Deservingness 

R Social Sciences Research Machine Learning

Honors thesis examining asylum adjudication outcomes across U.S. states.








2026 Fabio Bolz, James Hernandez, Teresa Pham



 Mild Cognitive Impairment Prediction 

R Machine Learning Healthcare

Comparative evaluation of random forest, SVM, LASSO, bagging, and neural network classifiers on the Wisconsin Longitudinal Study to identify early MCI. Addressed class imbalance via inverse-frequency weighting with clinical utility framing.








2024 Nibia Becerra Santillan, James Hernandez



 De-Venezuelization and Public Opinion in Colombia 

R Causal Inference Social Sciences

Difference-in-Differences analysis examining how President Petro’s “de-Venezuelization” approach shifted Colombian public attitudes toward Venezuelan migrants, using Latinobarómetro survey data from 2018–2023 and socioeconomic indicators from UN ECLAC.








2023 James Hernandez, Linh Nguyen, Na Nguyen



 Pokemon Card Game 

Java Computer Science

A multi-level memory card game to enhance concentration and critical thinking skills in 5-8 youth








2024 James Hernandez, Maria Sanchez-Linares, Tu Tran



 Chefbot: Recipe Recommender System 

Java Computer Science

Created a conversational recommender system that combines natural language interaction with machine learning-based recommendation techniques to provide personalized recipe suggestions from user-specified ingredients and preferences.

All Machine Learning Biostatistics Causal Inference Public Health Social Sciences Java Computer Science Research

title: Personal
text:
While much of this website focuses on my academic and professional work, I also enjoy activities that keep me active and curious!
My interests often revolve around exploration and personal growth—whether that means working through a difficult bouldering route, exploring a new city by bicycle, experimenting in the kitchen, or getting immersed in a well-designed game world.





My first time bouldering was from pure boredom on a random day in study abroad, and I had no idea what climbing really was. Now, it is my favorite hobby, and I the combination of physical strength, technique, and problem-solving that climbing builds. It’s also a great sport for socializing!



I really enjoy immersing myself in role-playing and strategy video games, particularly those with strong world-building and player choice. The Elder Scrolls series has long been a favorite, starting with Skyrim as a kid!



Growing up in a family where food was a love language, I’ve always enjoyed cooking and see it as a way to care for both the body and the spirit.
My favorite thing to bake is rosquillas, a traditional Nicaraguan cookie made from corn, cheese, and panela.



I read a mix of nonfiction and fiction, viewing both as ways to understand our world.
My nonfiction reading tends to focus on society and public issues. I’m currently reading Pathogenesis, which explores how microbiology has shaped human history and social change.
I also enjoy fiction, especially authors like Gabriel García Márquez, George Orwell, and Suzanne Collins, whose work explores politics, history, and human behavior through literary narrative.












I was born and raised in Louisville, Kentucky, best known for its bourbon, the Kentucky Derby, and fried chicken! While I currently call Minneapolis home, Louisville will always be a key part of my identity.








Though I moved to Minnesota in 2021 for school, I quickly fell in love with the Twin Cities, and am proud to now call myself a Minnesotan (though I have yet to try Hotdish).








Statues of “Las Gordas” can be found in Estelí, a mountainous northern department of Nicaragua, where my family is from. They celebrate abundance, fertility, and the strength of rural women.








I spent one semester studying at Universidad Carlos III Madrid, and fell in love with one of the most beautiful cities while exploring a very different academic and cultural environment.








On one of my trips back home, I was able to climb at the Red River Gorge, one of the most famous climbing destinations. As per tradition, I ate Miguel’s Pizza while drinking Ale-8 Soda - two Kentucky classics.








Hiking to La Chorrera, Colombia’s tallest waterfall, reminded me how much I enjoy spending time outdoors. Whether close to home or abroad, I always enjoy spending time explore nature.








Exploring the coastline around Naples and Mount Vesuvius was incredible, but visiting Pompeii was especially unbelievable. I’ve always been fascinated by history, and seeing an ancient city preserved for nearly two thousand years was an incredible experience.








The most effective way to test the quality and freshness of apples at an orchard.
